publications.title: T cells discriminate between groups C1 and C2 HLA-C.
publications.authors: Sim MJW, Stotz Z, Lu J, Brennan P, Long EO, Sun PD.
publications.executive_summary: This study examined how the C1/C2 dimorphism at HLA-C positions 77 and 80 affects T cell recognition, using KRAS-G12D neoantigen-specific TCRs presented on C1 and C2 HLA-C allotypes that were otherwise identical. The researchers found that TCRs discriminated between C1 and C2 despite both presenting the same KRAS-G12D peptide, with structural analysis revealing that position 77 (Ser in C1, Asn in C2) alters the peptide-binding pocket's preference for C-terminal amino acids, particularly at position pΩ-1, where C1 favors small residues and C2 prefers large ones. This difference in peptide preferences reduced TCR affinity for KRAS-G12D on C2-HLA-C even though TCR contact residues were conserved, demonstrating that the C1/C2 dimorphism independently modulates peptide presentation and T cell responses. The finding has direct implications for adoptive T cell therapy targeting KRAS-G12D-driven cancers, as TCRs selected or engineered on one HLA-C background may show reduced efficacy when presented on the alternative C1/C2 allotype in treated patients.
